subSlides.text: In 1993, John Meriwether assembled the most credentialed team in financial history. His partners included two future Nobel laureates in economics — Myron Scholes and Robert Merton — along with a former Federal Reserve vice chairman. Long-Term Capital Management was not merely a hedge fund. It was a test of whether modern financial theory could generate risk-free profits from mathematical certainty.

The theory was elegant. LTCM identified tiny price discrepancies between similar securities — bonds that should theoretically trade at the same price but didn't. By simultaneously buying the cheaper and selling the more expensive, the fund would profit when prices converged, regardless of which direction markets moved. The risk was supposed to be zero.

To convert tiny discrepancies into large profits, LTCM borrowed aggressively. At its peak, the fund had one hundred and twenty-five billion dollars in assets against a capital base of seven billion — a leverage ratio of twenty-five to one. Its off-balance-sheet derivatives exposure exceeded one trillion dollars.

Between 1994 and 1997, LTCM returned forty percent annually after fees. The strategy seemed to work. Banks competed to lend it money. The fund's models said its probability of losing everything in a year was one in ten to the twenty-fourth power — impossible in any timescale relevant to human civilization.

In August 1998, Russia defaulted on its domestic debt and devalued the ruble. The Russian crisis should not have directly affected LTCM, which had minimal Russian exposure. But financial crises generate a phenomenon the models had not adequately priced: correlation breakdown. In a panic, assets that normally move independently move together.

Investors worldwide fled to safety simultaneously. As prices diverged further rather than converging, LTCM's positions moved against it. Losses accelerated: five hundred million dollars in a single day in August 1998. By September, LTCM had lost ninety percent of its capital.

Its remaining four hundred million dollars supported one hundred and twenty-five billion in assets and one trillion in derivatives. If LTCM failed in a disorderly way — forced to sell everything simultaneously — it would move markets so violently that it could trigger failures at every major bank that had lent it money.

The core problem was a category error in risk measurement. LTCM's models measured risk as volatility — how much prices normally fluctuated. But the fund's true risk was tail risk: the probability that all its positions moved against it simultaneously in an extreme market event. Normal volatility was low. Tail risk was catastrophic.

Value-at-Risk models accurately predicted losses ninety-nine percent of the time. The one percent they failed to predict was the one percent that mattered. A model that works most of the time and fails catastrophically the rest is not a risk management system. It is a confidence-generating illusion.

In normal markets, leverage amplifies returns. In a liquidity crisis, leverage amplifies losses without limit. A twenty-five-to-one levered fund that experiences a four percent loss against its assets loses one hundred percent of its equity. Mathematics that is favorable in normal times becomes lethal in extremes.

LTCM had convinced itself that its positions were independent — that losses in one area would be cushioned by gains in another. But in August 1998, investors did not distinguish between strategies. They sold everything that was not a US Treasury. LTCM's supposedly uncorrelated positions all moved against it simultaneously.

LTCM's strategy, once it became known to be profitable, was copied by dozens of banks and funds. When the unwind began, everyone was trying to exit the same positions simultaneously. There were no buyers. Prices did not converge — they diverged, violently, because the entire industry was on the same side of the trade.

The systemic risk was hidden in plain sight. No single regulator had visibility into LTCM's total exposure because derivatives positions were not reported to any central authority. Each counterparty bank knew its own exposure but not the aggregate. The Federal Reserve knew something was wrong only when it was almost too late.

The lesson about complexity was fundamental: when a system is too complex for anyone to fully understand, and when that system is leveraged, the question is not whether it will fail but when. LTCM's one trillion in derivatives exposure was a system no single institution — including LTCM itself — could fully model.

On September 23, 1998, officials from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York gathered fourteen banks — LTCM's major creditors — in a room and told them the situation. If LTCM failed in a disorderly way, every bank in that room would face massive losses. Each had an incentive to liquidate first. If all did, the spiral would be unstoppable.

The Fed did not use public money. Instead, it facilitated a private bailout: the fourteen banks agreed to inject three billion six hundred million dollars into LTCM in exchange for ninety percent ownership, allowing an orderly unwinding of positions over the following year. The fund was not rescued — its investors lost everything. But markets were not destroyed.

The key insight was coordination. Each bank individually preferred to be the first to liquidate. Collectively, simultaneous liquidation was catastrophic. The Fed's role was to make the banks act as a collective rather than a collection of individuals. This is one of the few things central banks can do that markets cannot.

The Federal Reserve also cut interest rates three times in autumn 1998, providing liquidity to financial markets at a moment of extreme stress. The cuts were aimed at stabilising market conditions, not responding to economic weakness. This was new: using monetary policy to address a financial crisis rather than an economic slowdown.

Congress held hearings. Proposals for hedge fund regulation circulated. The President's Working Group on Financial Markets recommended greater transparency in derivatives markets. The recommendations were not implemented. The crisis passed quickly enough that the political urgency dissipated before the regulatory urgency could be translated into law.

The LTCM resolution was declared a success: no public money spent, systemic crisis averted, orderly unwind achieved. This success was its own danger. The message received by financial institutions was that extreme leverage and opacity were manageable risks because the Federal Reserve would coordinate a solution in a crisis. The lesson that should have been drawn was not drawn.

LTCM was wound down by 2000. Its investors lost ninety cents on the dollar. The fund that had made four hundred million dollars in fees in 1997 had destroyed four billion dollars in value by the time it closed. Its principals started new funds using similar strategies. The industry concluded the problem had been bad luck, not bad models.

The broader financial system recovered quickly. By early 1999, markets had stabilised and the technology bubble was in full expansion. The memory of the crisis faded rapidly. The rapid recovery was precisely the problem — it removed the urgency to reform the conditions that had created the crisis.

The phrase 'too big to fail' — previously applied to industrial companies — entered the vocabulary of finance. Systemic risk was acknowledged as a real phenomenon: an institution could be dangerous to the entire financial system not because of its own size but because of its interconnections with others.

The regulatory legacy was almost nonexistent. Derivatives remained opaque. Leverage limits were not imposed. Hedge fund transparency was not required. The models that had failed at LTCM — Value-at-Risk, normal distribution assumptions — remained the industry standard for measuring risk at every major bank. The 2008 crisis would demonstrate the cost of this failure.

What the world should have learned: mathematical sophistication is not risk management. Leverage can destroy institutions that are correct about the direction of markets but wrong about the timing. The regulatory frameworks built for transparent, exchange-traded securities are inadequate for opaque, bilaterally negotiated derivatives.

These lessons were articulated clearly in 1998. They were not applied until 2010, and only partially then. LTCM was a dress rehearsal for 2008. The audience watched the rehearsal, declared it a success, and forgot to change the script.

LTCM's models had been built using data from the period before the Asian crisis (Episode 10) had fully resolved — which meant the correlation assumptions underpinning every position were calibrated on a market that no longer existed. The hedge against the last crisis is rarely the hedge for the next one.
